examples.explanation: Use length 4 (power of two). Compute C[k] = sum_{i xor j = k} A[i]B[j]. Accumulating all pairs gives C[0]=5, C[1]=1, C[2]=5, C[3]=1, so the XOR convolution is [5,1,5,1].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Directly compute the XOR convolution by iterating over all pairs of elements from the two input arrays, applying the XOR operation and summing results. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs, suitable only for small problem sizes or educational purposes.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) if output is stored in-place, or O(n) for storing the result array

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force XOR Convolution
ladders.desc: Compute XOR convolution of two arrays by iterating over all element pairs.
ladders.explanation: The XOR convolution problem takes two arrays A and B of length n and produces an array C of length n where C[k] = Σ_{i XOR j = k} A[i] * B[j]. The brute force approach directly implements this definition by checking all possible pairs (i, j) from 0 to n-1. For each pair, we compute i XOR j to determine which position k in the result array should accumulate the product A[i] * B[j]. This is essentially a triple nested operation: outer loop over i, inner loop over j, and accumulation based on XOR result. The intuition is straightforward: we're explicitly enumerating all contributions to each output position as defined by the convolution formula. This approach works because it directly implements the mathematical definition without any optimizations. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize result array C of size n with zeros. 2. For i from 0 to n-1: 3.   For j from 0 to n-1: 4.     k = i XOR j 5.     C[k] += A[i] * B[j] 6. Return C. Time complexity is O(n²) since we have nested loops over n elements each. Space complexity is O(n) for the result array (or O(1) if we modify in-place, but typically we need to return a new array). Connection to higher levels: This is the complete implementation of the approach. Common mistakes include: forgetting to initialize the result array, using wrong loop bounds, misunderstanding XOR operation (it's bitwise, not logical), and integer overflow with large products. The brute force nature makes it only suitable for small n (typically n ≤ 1000). Real code pattern: def xor_convolution_brute(A, B): n = len(A); C = [0]*n; for i in range(n): for j in range(n): k = i ^ j; C[k] += A[i] * B[j]; return C

examples.trace: Take n=4 and evaluate all pairs (i,j), adding A[i]*B[j] to C[i xor j]. The accumulated result is C=[5,1,5,1].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Pairwise XOR and Accumulate
ladders.desc: For fixed i, iterate over all j to accumulate products into result based on XOR.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core computational kernel of the brute force approach. Given a fixed index i from array A, we iterate through all indices j of array B, compute k = i XOR j, and add A[i] * B[j] to the result array at position k. The intuition is that for each element A[i], we need to combine it with every element B[j] and route the product to the appropriate output slot determined by the XOR of their indices. This captures one 'row' of the convolution matrix. Step-by-step: 1. Input: arrays A, B, result array C (initialized to zeros), and current index i. 2. For j from 0 to n-1: 3.   k = i XOR j 4.   C[k] += A[i] * B[j] 5. No return value (C is modified in-place). Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n) since it loops over j. Space complexity is O(1) extra besides inputs. Connection to level 0: This sub-routine is called for each i in the outer loop of the full solution. Common mistakes: not using bitwise XOR (^ in most languages), forgetting to use multiplication for product, or incorrectly indexing C. Real code pattern: def accumulate_for_i(A, B, C, i): n = len(A); for j in range(n): k = i ^ j; C[k] += A[i] * B[j]

ladders.title: Core Logic — XOR-Based Index Routing
ladders.desc: Use XOR operation to determine which output index gets each product contribution.
ladders.explanation: The central insight of XOR convolution is that the output index k for a pair (i, j) is determined by the bitwise XOR of i and j: k = i XOR j. This is a mathematical property that defines the convolution. The intuition comes from group theory: XOR acts as addition in the group of bit vectors modulo 2, making this a convolution over the XOR group. Practically, XOR has the property that i XOR j = k if and only if i XOR k = j, which ensures symmetry. Step-by-step: Given i and j as integers, compute their binary representations, apply XOR bit-by-bit (output 1 if bits differ, 0 if same), and interpret the result as an integer k. This k tells us where to accumulate the product A[i]*B[j]. For example, if i=3 (binary 11) and j=1 (01), XOR gives 10 which is 2, so product goes to C[2]. Time/space: Computing XOR is O(1) time and space. Connection to level 1: This logic is used inside the inner loop to compute k. Common mistakes: confusing XOR with other bitwise operations like AND or OR, or not understanding that indices are treated as binary numbers. Real code pattern: k = i ^ j  # in Python

examples.trace: Binary: 0 XOR 0 = 0. In bits: 0^0=0.
Binary: 1 is 01, 2 is 10. XOR: 01^10=11 which is 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate Over All Index Pairs
ladders.desc: Generate all possible (i, j) pairs where i and j range from 0 to n-1.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating nested loops to iterate over every combination of indices i from array A and j from array B. For arrays of length n, there are n² such pairs. The intuition is that to compute the convolution, we need to consider every interaction between elements of A and B. This is a fundamental pattern in many brute force algorithms. Step-by-step: 1. Outer loop: for i in range(n): 2. Inner loop: for j in range(n): 3. Process pair (i, j). Time complexity is O(n²) for the iteration itself. Space complexity is O(1) for loop counters. Connection to level 2: Once we have a pair (i, j), we apply XOR to get k. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (using < n or <= n-1), or using wrong variable names. Real code pattern: for i in range(n): for j in range(n): # process i, j

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bitwise XOR Computation
ladders.desc: Compute the XOR of two integers bit by bit.
ladders.explanation: Bitwise XOR (exclusive OR) is a fundamental operation that takes two bits and returns 1 if they are different, 0 if they are the same. For integers, XOR is applied to each corresponding bit of their binary representations. The intuition is that XOR captures the 'difference' between two bit patterns. For example, 5 (101) XOR 3 (011) = 6 (110) because: 1^0=1, 0^1=1, 1^1=0. Step-by-step: 1. Convert integers to binary (padded to same length). 2. For each bit position, compare the two bits: if different, output 1; if same, output 0. 3. Combine bits back into an integer. In programming, this is done directly with the ^ operator. Time/space: O(1) assuming fixed-size integers. Connection to level 3: After getting pair (i, j), compute i XOR j. Common mistakes: confusing XOR with logical OR (|) or AND (&), or not understanding bitwise vs logical operations. Real code pattern: result = a ^ b

examples.trace: 6 binary 110, 3 binary 011. Bitwise: 1^0=1, 1^1=0, 0^1=1. Result 101 which is 5.
10 binary 1010, 12 binary 1100. Align: 1010 ^ 1100 = 0110 which is 6.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Loops
ladders.desc: Access array elements using indices and iterate with loops.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves understanding how to work with arrays: accessing elements by their index (position) and using loops to traverse all elements. An array is a collection of items stored at contiguous memory locations, each accessible via an integer index starting from 0. A loop (like for or while) allows repeating a block of code multiple times. The intuition is that to process all elements in an array, we need to visit each one sequentially using its index. Step-by-step: 1. Declare an array, e.g., arr = [10, 20, 30]. 2. Access element at index i: value = arr[i]. 3. Use a loop to go through all indices: for i in range(len(arr)): process arr[i]. Time complexity for traversing an array of size n is O(n). Space complexity is O(1) for the loop counter. Connection to level 4: We use indexing to get A[i] and B[j], and loops to iterate over i and j. Common mistakes: using 1-based indexing (many languages use 0-based), going out of bounds (index >= length), or misunderstanding loop ranges. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)): print(arr[i])

examples.trace: Index 1 refers to the second element (since indexing starts at 0). So arr[1] = 10.

examples.input: arr=[2,4,6,8], loop to print all elements
examples.trace: for i in range(4): print(arr[i]). i=0: print 2; i=1: print 4; i=2: print 6; i=3: print 8.

approaches.name: Fast Walsh-Hadamard Transform O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Use the Fast Walsh-Hadamard Transform (FWHT) to compute the XOR convolution efficiently by transforming the input arrays into the Walsh-Hadamard domain, performing pointwise multiplication, and then inverse transforming back. This exploits the convolution theorem for XOR, reducing the problem from quadratic to linearithmic time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — XOR Convolution via Fast Walsh-Hadamard Transform
ladders.desc: Compute the XOR convolution of two arrays using FWHT in O(n log n) time.
ladders.explanation: The XOR convolution problem requires computing c[k] = Σ a[i] * b[j] for all i XOR j = k, where a and b are arrays of length n (power of two). Direct computation takes O(n²) time. The Fast Walsh-Hadamard Transform (FWHT) solves this efficiently by exploiting the convolution theorem for XOR: the XOR convolution in the original domain corresponds to pointwise multiplication in the Walsh-Hadamard domain. This works because the Walsh-Hadamard transform diagonalizes the XOR operation. The algorithm has three phases: 1) Transform both arrays using FWHT (O(n log n)), 2) Multiply the transformed arrays element-wise (O(n)), 3) Inverse transform the result (O(n log n)). The intuition is that transforming to the Walsh-Hadamard basis makes the convolution operation separable into independent components. The FWHT is implemented recursively: for an array of size n, split into halves, recursively transform each half, then combine using butterfly operations with coefficients +1 and -1. The inverse transform is identical except for dividing by n at the end. Time complexity is O(n log n) due to log n recursive levels each processing n elements. Space complexity is O(n) for the transformed arrays. Common mistakes include not ensuring n is a power of two (pad with zeros), forgetting to divide by n in inverse transform, and incorrect butterfly coefficients. This connects to L1 by using the transform/inverse transform subroutines.

examples.trace: Step 1: Ensure n=2 (power of two). Step 2: Transform a via FWHT: H(a) = [1+2, 1-2] = [3, -1]. Step 3: Transform b: H(b) = [3+4, 3-4] = [7, -1]. Step 4: Pointwise multiply: [3*7, -1*-1] = [21, 1]. Step 5: Inverse transform: Apply FWHT to [21, 1]: [21+1, 21-1] = [22, 20], then divide by n=2: [11, 10]. But wait, verify: Direct convolution: c[0]=a[0]*b[0]+a[1]*b[1]=1*3+2*4=3+8=11, c[1]=a[0]*b[1]+a[1]*b[0]=1*4+2*3=4+6=10. Output should be [11, 10]. Correction: Inverse transform gives [22, 20], divide by 2 = [11, 10]. So output is [11, 10].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Fast Walsh-Hadamard Transform and Inverse
ladders.desc: Implement the in-place FWHT to transform an array to/from Walsh-Hadamard domain.
ladders.explanation: The Fast Walsh-Hadamard Transform (FWHT) is a linear transformation that maps an array of size n (power of two) to another array of same size. For XOR convolution, it serves as the change of basis that diagonalizes the XOR operation. The transform is defined recursively: for array A of length n, split into two halves A0 (first n/2) and A1 (last n/2). Recursively transform A0 and A1. Then combine: for i from 0 to n/2-1, compute newA[i] = A0[i] + A1[i] and newA[i+n/2] = A0[i] - A1[i]. This is the butterfly operation. The inverse transform is identical except the result is divided by n. Intuition: The transform decomposes the array into components that are invariant under XOR, allowing convolution to become pointwise multiplication. The algorithm runs in O(n log n) time because each recursive level processes n elements and there are log n levels. Space is O(n) for the array. Common mistakes: not handling non-power-of-two sizes (should pad with zeros), incorrect indexing in butterfly, forgetting to divide in inverse. This connects to L0 as the core transformation used in the convolution solution. Real code pattern: function fwht(arr, invert=False): n=len(arr); h=1; while h<n: for i in range(0,n,2*h): for j in range(i,i+h): x=arr[j]; y=arr[j+h]; arr[j]=x+y; arr[j+h]=x-y; h*=2; if invert: for i in range(n): arr[i]/=n.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=2, h=1. Step 2: i=0, j=0: x=arr[0]=1, y=arr[1]=2. arr[0]=1+2=3, arr[1]=1-2=-1. Step 3: h becomes 2, loop ends. Output [3, -1].

Step 1: Apply forward transform first (same as above): n=2, h=1, i=0, j=0: x=3, y=-1, arr[0]=3+(-1)=2, arr[1]=3-(-1)=4. Step 2: Now invert: since invert=True, divide each element by n=2: arr[0]=2/2=1, arr[1]=4/2=2. Output [1, 2].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pointwise Multiplication in Transformed Domain
ladders.desc: Multiply corresponding elements of two transformed arrays to compute convolution.
ladders.explanation: After transforming two arrays A and B using FWHT to get A' and B', the XOR convolution C is obtained by computing C'[i] = A'[i] * B'[i] for all i, then inverse transforming C'. This works due to the convolution theorem for XOR: the transform diagonalizes the convolution operation, meaning that in the transformed domain, convolution reduces to simple element-wise multiplication. Intuition: The Walsh-Hadamard basis vectors are eigenvectors of the XOR operation, so convolution in the original space corresponds to scaling these independent components in the transformed space. The multiplication is straightforward: iterate through indices 0 to n-1 and multiply the corresponding elements. Time complexity is O(n) as it's a single loop over n elements. Space complexity is O(n) for storing the result array. Common mistakes: mismatched array lengths (should be same size), not using the transformed arrays (must apply FWHT first). This connects to L1 as the step performed after transformation and before inverse transformation. Real code pattern: result = [0]*n; for i in range(n): result[i] = A_transformed[i] * B_transformed[i].

examples.trace: Step 1: n=2. Step 2: For i=0: result[0] = 3 * 7 = 21. Step 3: For i=1: result[1] = (-1) * (-1) = 1. Output [21, 1].

ladders.title: Building Block — Butterfly Operation in FWHT
ladders.desc: Implement the butterfly step that combines two halves of an array during FWHT.
ladders.explanation: The butterfly operation is the core computational step in the Fast Walsh-Hadamard Transform. It combines two halves of an array using addition and subtraction. Given an array A of length n (power of two), after recursively transforming its two halves A0 (first n/2 elements) and A1 (last n/2 elements), the butterfly computes: for i from 0 to n/2-1, newA[i] = A0[i] + A1[i] and newA[i+n/2] = A0[i] - A1[i]. Intuition: This operation mixes the transformed halves to produce the full transformed array, corresponding to combining basis vectors in the Walsh-Hadamard transform. It's called a butterfly because of the pattern of additions and subtractions resembling butterfly wings in signal processing diagrams. The operation is linear and invertible (the inverse uses the same pattern with division). Time complexity for one butterfly on an array of size n is O(n) as it loops through n/2 pairs. Space is O(1) extra if done in-place. Common mistakes: incorrect indexing leading to out-of-bounds errors, not handling odd sizes (should only be called for powers of two). This connects to L2 as the fundamental step used repeatedly in the transform. Real code pattern: for i in range(n//2): x = A0[i]; y = A1[i]; A[i] = x + y; A[i+n//2] = x - y.

examples.trace: Step 1: n/2=1. Step 2: i=0: x=A0[0]=1, y=A1[0]=2. newA[0]=1+2=3, newA[1]=1-2=-1. Output array of length 2: [3, -1].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Splitting and Recursive Processing
ladders.desc: Split an array into halves and process recursively, a fundamental divide-and-conquer step.
ladders.explanation: This operation involves taking an array of length n (assumed power of two) and dividing it into two equal halves: the first half (indices 0 to n/2-1) and the second half (indices n/2 to n-1). This is a key step in divide-and-conquer algorithms like FWHT. Intuition: By splitting the problem into smaller subproblems, we can solve them independently and then combine results efficiently. The splitting is done by simply taking slices of the array. After splitting, each half is processed recursively (e.g., transformed via FWHT), and then the results are combined using a butterfly operation. Time complexity for splitting is O(n) if copying arrays, but can be O(1) if using indices to represent subarrays. Space complexity depends on implementation; recursive calls use stack space O(log n). Common mistakes: not handling base case (n=1), incorrect half sizes for odd n (should ensure n is power of two), deep recursion causing stack overflow for large n (can be avoided with iterative implementation). This connects to L3 as the preparatory step before applying the butterfly. Real code pattern: mid = n//2; left = arr[:mid]; right = arr[mid:]; process(left); process(right); combine(left, right).

examples.trace: Step 1: n=4, mid=4//2=2. Step 2: left = arr[0:2] = [1,2]. Step 3: right = arr[2:4] = [3,4]. Output two arrays.

Step 1: n=1, mid=1//2=0. Step 2: left = arr[0:0] = []? Wait, for n=1, base case: no splitting needed, but if forced: left = arr[:0]=[], right = arr[0:]=[5]. Typically, recursive base case handles n=1 directly.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — XOR Operation and Array Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand the XOR bitwise operation and how to access array elements by index.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers two basics: 1) The XOR (exclusive OR) operation, denoted ^ in many languages, is a bitwise operation that returns 1 if the bits are different, 0 if same. For example, 1 XOR 0 = 1, 0 XOR 0 = 0. It's commutative and associative. In the context of arrays, indices are non-negative integers, and XOR between indices is used to define the convolution condition i XOR j = k. 2) Array indexing: accessing an element in an array using its position (index), starting from 0. For an array A of length n, valid indices are 0 to n-1. Intuition: XOR convolution relies on pairing indices whose XOR equals a target index, so understanding XOR is crucial. Array indexing is needed to store and retrieve values. Time and space: XOR is O(1) per operation, array access is O(1). Common mistakes: confusing XOR with other bitwise operations (like OR or AND), off-by-one errors in indexing (e.g., accessing index n). This connects to L4 as arrays are the data structure being split and processed. Real code pattern: result = a ^ b; value = arr[index].

examples.trace: Step 1: Convert to binary: 5 = 101, 3 = 011. Step 2: Perform XOR bit by bit: 1 XOR 0 = 1, 0 XOR 1 = 1, 1 XOR 1 = 0. Step 3: Result binary = 110, which is decimal 6.
Step 1: Array indices: 0 -> 10, 1 -> 20, 2 -> 30. Step 2: Index 1 corresponds to the second element. Step 3: Output 20.
